Proprioception is housed in ligament mechanoreceptors — every sprain degrades balance and rapid-adjustment for weeks after the acute injury heals.
Proactive proprioceptive training is the single highest-value injury-prevention investment for basketball.
Returning from sprain too early — swelling resolves before proprioceptive capacity does
Rectus abdominis + obliques + erector spinae produce and resist spinal movement in multiple planes — the engine of every trunk-driven basketball action.
"Draw in the abdominals" first — inner-core activation precedes outer-core loading; without it, outer-core work leaks stability.
Neglecting anti-extension and anti-rotation training
Largest muscle in the body — greatest force-production capacity; training it lifts every explosive basketball action.
Horizontal and vertical propulsion — hip thrust for horizontal (driving past), squat for vertical (jumping).
Quadriceps-dominant training only (squats with hip-flexor bias)
Mobility before strength — a tight iliopsoas limits stride length and baseline-cut depth before strength ever becomes the limiter.
Reciprocal with the glutes — chronically tight hip flexors inhibit gluteal activation; training mobility here unlocks the posterior chain.
Flexion strength only (leg raises, sit-ups) without mobility work
Quiet but fast feet — loud landings indicate too much ground contact time; emphasize the push-off, not just the landing.
Breaks the ankle's elastic rhythm; defeats the rapid-contact target
Hip hinge only — all motion comes from the hip joint; the upper torso stays stable and rigid.
Do not go beyond parallel to the floor or arch the spine — hyperextension places dangerous stress on the vertebrae.
Reduces target loading; if seen, shorten the range of motion
Stay in the squat — don't stand up between steps; keep tension on the band at all times.
Takes tension off the lateral hip; defeats the propulsion target
"Carpenter's level" — a level across the shoulders and across the hips must stay perfectly flat; no tilting.
Hip or shoulder rotation during arm/leg extension
Most common error; reduce hold time before adding reps
Drive through the hips, not the lower back — extension comes from the hips, not the low back.
Back of shoulder firm on padded bench; cervical spine (neck) must NOT be the main support.
Transfers load off the glute and into the lower back
Point guard penetrates without help; kick-out passes produce wide-open 3-pointers. Correction: corner defenders must be up near the elbows.
Quick slip pass over the top for an easy catch-and-score. Correction: stay one or two steps above your man.
One side is 3-on-2 or 4-on-3. Correction: the four off-ball defenders must all shift to cover elbows and boxes on the ball side.
Drive to the lane; no help available. Correction: force sideline; never let the ball go middle in this set.
X4 losing Player 4 under the cross screen
X4 must beat Player 4 to the spot; playing behind creates an easy low-post feed.
X5 failing to pre-rotate before the pass
A late rotation leaves the paint open; X5 must move as the trap forms, not when the ball is released.
After helping on Player 3's penetration, X2 must sprint back to Player 2 on the weak side or risk an open three.
If X4 follows the roll when Player 5 actually pops, the open three-pointer is gifted; read the roller's body language immediately.
Playing on top of the screen recipient with back to ball
gives the offensive player a step-up or cut option; always face the ball
Screener's defender chasing the screener after he cuts
gives the screen recipient a clean path to his spot
Ball defender not pressuring after the pass
cutter receives ball with space to drive or shoot; ball defender must drop to nail immediately
the handoff screen is a trap for defenders who lose their positioning; must go through/inside
the trap must occur at the moment of the handoff — late trapping lets the receiver get a step before help arrives
when trapping, the passer can slip away for a backdoor or dive to the basket; weak-side help must account for this
receiver gets a free catch-and-shoot; correction: communicate early and go through inside
Both defenders following the ball on the switch
backdoor cut goes uncovered; correction: third defender stays alert to the cutter
Defender retreating too much on get action
gives the passer a running start on the return cut; correction: step back only enough to allow the through-go
The lob over the front goes unchecked for an easy layup; X5 must anticipate and close before the pass.
X3/X4/X2 must maintain awareness of both the ball and their assignments in the help rotation.
If the opponent's best player catches cleanly at the low post, the defense is in trouble; fronting is preferable for elite scorers.
Easy catch at the elbow opens up easy scores for a skilled big. Correction: overplay and contest all flash-post entries.
Wing defender watches the ball instead of man
Wing slips backdoor for an easy layup. Correction: *"keep his man in front of him if possible"* — always maintain man awareness.
Backdoor cutter gets an easy layup off the flash. Correction: the low-block defender MUST cross the lane on any backdoor attempt.
Quick pass to the weak side corner produces open shots. Correction: nail, elbow, box must be occupied immediately.
Offense gets a layup. Correction: Low-block defender has an absolute assignment to cross the lane on flash-post action. [S1, p.148]
Post defender attacks the ball instead of passing lanes
Passer finds a cutter or open perimeter player. Correction: Step back, widen, and control passing lanes rather than lunging at ball handler. [S1, p.148]
Wing defender chases his man and vacates passing lane
Gut pass reaches the middle. Correction: Open up, retreat to goal, or snap back rather than losing floor position. [S1, p.148]
screens your own teammate; correction: bump, step out, hold up
Playing on top of down-screen receiver with back to the ball
gives him cutting/step-up options; correction: always move toward the ball
fader catches open; correction: recognize the fade early and shoot the gap
cutter's defender has no help; correction: screener's defender must show on every screen
cutters can use screens at full speed; correction: make contact with the screener before he is set
Passer's defender gets screened off and cutter receives an easy layup pass. Correction: post defender must step back and create a clear lane before the cutter arrives.
Ball handler gets a clean pull-up jumper at the elbow. Correction: fight over all pinch-post screens.
Post player dribbles into the paint unopposed. Correction: nail, elbow, and box must be filled immediately on any pinch-post action.
Middle defender leaves his zone and the pass splits the defense. Correction: force all passes over the outside shoulder of the trapper.
One cutter goes free; the switch is split. Correction: Back defender must call it early and loudly — "screen, switch!" [S1, p.149]
Quick passes exploit depth disadvantage. Correction: Both defenders must adjust upward on the pass to the post so they are already in position when the split begins. [S1, p.149]
Offensive player dribbles through the middle of the switch. Correction: Both defenders must come together aggressively and not allow any splitting of the seam. [S1, p.149]
Losing sight of the ball while guarding a weak-side cutter
stay level with the ball, use peripheral vision, ball-you-man principle
screener's defender must never overrun and pick off their own teammate
soft defense allows easy low-block catches and cuts to the goal
every play in this system requires maximum ball pressure to succeed
Post defender not fronting when ball is at or below FT line
three-quartering gives the lock-and-lob opportunity
Weak-side defenders not being drawn to the ball
level with ball must be the default, not sagging too deep
Going over the first screen too aggressively
cutter shortcuts under both screens for an easy catch; must trail first and shortcut second
Screener's defenders not standing up screeners
cutter gets clean cuts; screeners must be held up
if the cutter's defender gets screened on the second screen, silence = open shot; must verbally call "switch"
staggered screens are designed to create a catch-and-shoot; the passer must be pressured to disrupt timing
